record.field: Keycloak is an open-source identity and access management platform that adds authentication and authorization to applications without writing your own user-management code.

Keycloak is an open-source identity and access management platform that adds authentication and authorization to applications without writing your own user-management code.

It centralizes login, user storage, and permissions behind standard protocols (OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0 and SAML 2.0), so applications delegate identity to a single trusted server. Originally created by Red Hat and now a CNCF project built on Quarkus, it targets developers and platform teams that need single sign-on, social and enterprise login, and fine-grained access control across many services.

Key features
  • Single Sign-On (SSO) and Single Logout across applications via OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0 and SAML 2.0
  • User federation with LDAP and Active Directory, plus social and external identity-provider brokering
  • Strong authentication: multi-factor (OTP, WebAuthn/passkeys), configurable login flows and password policies
  • Centralized user, role, group and client management through a web admin console and full REST Admin API
  • Fine-grained, policy-based authorization services for resource and scope-level access control
  • Customizable login themes, account self-service console, and multi-realm tenant isolation
Best for

Organizations and development teams that want to self-host a centralized identity provider for SSO, MFA and access control across internal and customer-facing applications, replacing per-app authentication with a single standards-based server.

services: postgres: image: postgres:16.2 environment: POSTGRES_DB: {{ postgres_db }} POSTGRES_USER: {{ postgres_user }} POSTGRES_PASSWORD: {{ postgres_password }} volumes: - ./postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data healthcheck: test: - CMD-SHELL - pg_isready -U $$POSTGRES_USER -d $$POSTGRES_DB interval: 10s timeout: 5s retries: 5 restart: always container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}_postgres' networks: - internal keycloak: image: quay.io/keycloak/keycloak:26.3.5 depends_on: postgres: condition: service_healthy environment: KC_DB: postgres KC_DB_URL: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/{{ postgres_db }} KC_DB_USERNAME: {{ postgres_user }} KC_DB_PASSWORD: {{ postgres_password }} KC_BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_USERNAME: {{ keycloak_admin }} KC_BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_PASSWORD: {{ keycloak_admin_password }} KC_HOSTNAME: {{ kc_hostname }} KC_HTTP_ENABLED: 'true' KC_HEALTH_ENABLED: 'true' KC_PROXY_HEADERS: xforwarded command: start-dev restart: always container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}' ports: - '{{ PORT_HTTP }}:8080' networks: - internal - agz-proxy-network networks: internal: driver: bridge agz-proxy-network: external: true

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Dump consistente de PostgreSQL (servicio en marcha) → $BACKUP_DATA_DIR docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_dump -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --no-owner -Fc' \ > "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump" echo "pg_dump OK ($(wc -c < "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump") bytes)"

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Levanta solo la DB, espera readiness y restaura docker compose up -d postgres for i in $(seq 1 30); do docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_isready -U "$POSTGRES_USER"' && break sleep 2 done docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_restore -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --clean --if-exists --no-owner' \ < "$RESTORE_DATA_DIR/db.dump" # Verificación: la DB no quedó vacía (sh -c con dobles comillas; $VAR del contenedor con \$) N=$(docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c "psql -U \$POSTGRES_USER -d \$POSTGRES_DB -tAc \"SELECT count(*) FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public'\"") echo "tablas restauradas: $N" test "$N" -gt 0
